title: Listening - Part 3: Farming in the Sky
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an agricultural technologist, Elliot, and a traditional farmer, Ada, about the rise of vertical farming in cities. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding the efficiency of hydroponics, the cost of LED lighting, and the future of food security.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: By 2050, two-thirds of the world will live in cities. How will we feed them? One answer is vertical farming—growing crops in stacked layers indoors. Joining me are Elliot, a pioneer in urban ag-tech, and Ada, who runs a large organic farm in the countryside. Elliot, why move farming into the city?

Elliot: [15] It’s about 'food miles' and 'resource efficiency.' Currently, a head of lettuce might travel two thousand miles before it reaches your plate, losing half its nutrients along the way. [/15] In a vertical farm, we grow it two miles from the consumer. We use ninety-five percent less water than traditional farming because we recycle every drop in a closed hydroponic system. There are no pesticides because the environment is sterile. It’s the ultimate controlled experiment.

Interviewer: Ada, you’ve been farming the land for thirty years. Is Elliot’s vision the future or a fantasy?

Ada: [16] I see the technical appeal, but I worry about the energy cost. You’re replacing the free, natural light of the sun with thousands of LED bulbs that have to be on eighteen hours a day. [/16] Unless that energy is one hundred percent renewable, you’re just trading a water problem for a massive carbon problem. Also, vertical farms are great for leafy greens and herbs, but you can't feed the world on kale. You can't grow wheat, corn, or soy in a skyscraper—at least not economically.

Interviewer: Elliot, is the energy bill the 'Achilles' heel' of your industry?

Elliot: [17] It was, but the efficiency of LEDs has tripled in the last decade. We are also integrating our farms into the 'circular economy' of the city. We can use the waste heat from data centers to warm our plants, and we can use the CO2 captured from city air to speed up growth. [/17] As for the crops, we are starting with greens because they are high-value, but we’re already experimenting with dwarf varieties of berries and even rice. The technology is still in its 'iPhone 1' stage.

Interviewer: Ada, what about the 'soul' of food? Does a tomato grown in a lab taste the same as one grown in soil?

Ada: [18] That’s the heart of the matter. Soil is a living community of fungi and bacteria that give food its 'terroir'—its specific flavor and character. When you grow in a sterile nutrient solution, you get a perfect-looking product that often tastes like nothing. [/18] We are treating food like a software product, but humans have a deep, biological need to be connected to the land. We shouldn't be separating ourselves further from nature.

Interviewer: Elliot, can vertical farms help with food security in a world of extreme weather?

Elliot: [19] That’s our strongest argument. Traditional farms are at the mercy of droughts, floods, and pests. A vertical farm is 'climate-proof.' We can produce a harvest every month of the year, regardless of what’s happening outside. [/19] In a future where the 'breadbaskets' of the world are failing due to climate change, these urban hubs will be our life insurance.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Ada? Is there a way for your two worlds to coexist?

Ada: [20] We need a hybrid model. Vertical farms should handle the perishables for the cities, while we focus on the large-scale staple crops and regenerative agriculture that restores the soil. It shouldn't be an 'either-or' choice. We need every tool we have to survive the next century. [/20]

Interviewer: Elliot, Ada, thank you for a very grounded—and elevated—discussion.

questions.question: What does Elliot identify as a major advantage of urban farming?
options: A) It allows for the use of more natural fertilizers.
B) It significantly reduces the distance that food must be transported.
C) It is the only way to grow organic produce at scale.
D) It provides employment for thousands of city dwellers.
questions.explanation: He mentions 'food miles', explaining that food is grown two miles from the consumer rather than traveling thousands of miles.

questions.question: Ada's main reservation about vertical farming is that
options: A) the plants are more likely to be affected by city pollution.
B) it uses too much water compared to traditional methods.
C) it requires a massive amount of electricity to power the lights.
D) there are no workers skilled enough to operate the systems.
questions.explanation: She worries about the 'energy cost' of replacing free sunlight with thousands of LED bulbs on for 18 hours a day.

questions.question: How does Elliot respond to the criticism regarding energy use?
options: A) He claims that electricity is becoming cheaper every year.
B) He suggests using energy from waste products and city infrastructure.
C) He argues that plants do not actually need that much light to grow.
D) He admits that the industry will always depend on fossil fuels.
questions.explanation: He mentions using waste heat from data centers and capturing CO2 from city air to integrate with the 'circular economy'.

questions.question: Regarding the quality of the food, Ada argues that
options: A) lab-grown food is actually more nutritious than soil-grown food.
B) the lack of soil results in a product that lacks complex flavor.
C) most consumers cannot tell the difference between the two types.
D) vertical farms should focus on growing meat instead of vegetables.
questions.explanation: She says soil gives food its 'terroir' and flavor, while sterile solutions produce something that 'often tastes like nothing'.

questions.question: Elliot believes that vertical farms are essential for the future because they
options: A) are cheaper to build than traditional farmhouses.
B) can provide a consistent food supply regardless of the weather.
C) allow cities to become completely independent from the countryside.
D) are the only way to prevent the spread of new crop diseases.
questions.explanation: He calls them 'climate-proof' and says they provide 'life insurance' when traditional 'breadbaskets' fail due to climate change.

questions.question: In her conclusion, Ada suggests that the best approach would be to
options: A) replace all traditional farms with vertical ones within twenty years.
B) focus only on the crops that are easiest to grow in skyscrapers.
C) use both vertical and traditional methods for different types of crops.
D) encourage more people to move back to the countryside and farm.
questions.explanation: She proposes a 'hybrid model' where vertical farms handle perishables and traditional farms focus on staples and soil health.
